beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Negative parallelism — 'it's not X, it's Y' — is the tell because every model reaches for it every time. Once readers spot the pattern, they stop reading your content and start reading you as a machine. The fix is one file, anti-ai-writing-style.md, encoding banned patterns, a hundred-word vocabulary ban, pacing rules, and tone. A global instruction makes Claude read your Cowork folder and audit for the pattern before every response.

props.artifactTitle: Negative parallelism is the AI tell
artifactLines: Negative parallelism — 'it's not X, it's Y' — is the tell because every model reaches for it every time.
Once readers spot the pattern, they stop reading your content and start reading you as a machine.
The fix is one file, anti-ai-writing-style.md, encoding banned patterns, a hundred-word vocabulary ban, pacing rules, and tone.
A global instruction makes Claude read your Cowork folder and audit for the pattern before every response.

beats.narration_text: The idea that negative parallelism makes readers stop reading the content and start reading the writer really lands for me. I want to build that anti-ai-writing-style.md file for my own voice. Help me (1) list negative parallelism plus a few of its fifteen disguises so I can recognize them mid-draft, (2) draft the hundred-word vocabulary ban tailored to the corporate clichés I overuse, and (3) write the global instruction that makes Claude read the file before every task automatically.

props.command: The idea that negative parallelism makes readers stop reading the content and start reading the writer really lands for me. I want to build that anti-ai-writing-style.md file for my own voice. Help me (1) list negative parallelism plus a few of its fifteen disguises so I can recognize them mid-draft, (2) draft the hundred-word vocabulary ban tailored to the corporate clichés I overuse, and (3) write the global instruction that makes Claude read the file before every task automatically.

beats.narration_text: Negative parallelism is the tell because it's not actually how humans write. It's how AI structures contrast — clean, symmetrical, confident. It's not a bug. It's what the model reaches for when it needs to make a point feel sharp. The problem is every model reaches for it, every time. So the pattern itself is now the signal. When a reader sees it, they stop reading the content and start reading the writer.

artifactLines: It's not [X], it's [Y]. — the original.
This isn't about [X], it's about [Y].
Not [X] — [Y].
Less [X], more [Y].
Forget [X]. Focus on [Y].
Stop [X]. Start [Y]. — and 10 more variants.
All of them: same shape, same AI fingerprint.

beats.narration_text: The fix is one file: anti-ai-writing-style.md. You create it once, drop it in your Claude Cowork folder, and it runs before every response. The file should encode banned sentence patterns, vocabulary constraints, pacing rules, and tone guidance. It contains: the banned sentence patterns — negative parallelism and 15 disguises — plus a hundred-word vocabulary ban, pacing rules, and tone guidelines. It doesn't ask Claude to be creative. It tells Claude what it's not allowed to reach for.

props.title: anti-ai-writing-style.md
props.code:
# BANNED PATTERNS
Negative parallelism and its 15 variants:
- "It's not [X], it's [Y]."
- "This isn't about X, it's about Y."
- "Not X — Y."
- "Less X, more Y."
→ Never use. Rewrite with direct assertion. # BANNED VOCABULARY (100+ words)
delve, unlock, leverage, empower, dive into,
landscape, robust, seamless, game-changer... # PACING RULES
Short sentences earn long ones. Not the reverse.

beats.narration_text: The workflow is three steps. Your Cowork folder has three files: about-me, my-company, and anti-ai-writing-style. Claude reads all three before every session because of a global instruction you paste once. Then when you ask for any written output, the audit runs automatically. The audit catches negative parallelism mid-draft and rewrites it on the spot. You can push further — type 'audit it harder' and Claude finds what it missed.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B03.png (screenshot of Cowork folder showing about-me, my-company, anti-ai-writing-style files — the folder setup from the article)

beats.narration_text: The global instruction is what makes it automatic. You paste it once into Claude's settings — it tells Claude: before any task, read my Cowork folder. Read about-me, anti-ai-writing-style, and my-company. Never touch the output or templates folders unless I say so. Save work in Outputs. If the brief is unclear, use AskUserQuestion. That's it. Every session inherits the rules without you typing a prompt.

props.title: global-instructions.md
props.code:
I usually start my Cowork session by pointing you to
my Cowork folder. Before any and every single task, you must read every
file in ABOUT ME/:
- about-me: who I am, what I love and hate
- anti-ai-writing-style: audit everything against it
- my-company: where I work, my role Never read OUTPUTS/ or TEMPLATES/ unless I point you.
Save deliverables in OUTPUTS/ under the project name. If the brief is unclear, use AskUserQuestion.
Don't over-explain. Deliver the work.

beats.narration_text: The vocabulary ban is where the file earns its weight. Over 100 words and phrases that every AI reaches for when it thinks it's being clear. Delve, unlock, leverage, empower, dive into, landscape, robust, seamless, game-changer, foster, streamline, holistic, paradigm, synergy. Not because they're wrong — because they're default. The audit catches them and rewrites with concrete language. Use, not leverage. Works at any size, not scalable.

artifactLines: leverage → use
scalable → works at any size
unlock → open, find, reach
dive into → read, look at, check
foster → build, grow, encourage
seamless → smooth, clean, simple
The rule: if it sounds like an AI, find the real word.

beats.narration_text: This is not a Claude-only fix. The file works in any AI. ChatGPT Projects, Gemini, Grok — upload the file and the audit runs the same way. Cowork is a strong environment for this workflow, but the anti-ai-writing-style file is portable. And you can manage it in Obsidian — free, local, Markdown-native — so every file in your Cowork folder is easy to update without hunting through Claude's settings.

artifactLines: Claude Cowork — drop it in your Cowork folder.
ChatGPT — upload to a Project.
Gemini — upload to a conversation or Gem.
Manage all your files in Obsidian (free, Markdown).
The audit runs wherever the file is.

beats.narration_text: In practice, the audit catches three classes of AI-ism. Structural — negative parallelism, and the 15 rhetorical patterns that work the same way. Lexical — the hundred words that flag AI-written text to any careful reader. And tonal — the over-explanation, the over-confidence, the warmth that wasn't asked for. Strip all three and what's left reads like a person wrote it, because a person constrained the machine.

artifactLines: Structural — negative parallelism and 15 variants.
Lexical — 100+ words no human reaches for first.
Tonal — over-explanation, unasked-for warmth, false confidence.
Strip all three: the text reads like a person.
Because a person constrained the machine.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Create an anti-AI-writing file, drop it in your Cowork folder, paste the global instruction once, and ask Claude to audit something you wrote last week. Paste this prompt — it handles the whole workflow.

props.command: Read the anti-ai-writing-style.md in my folder. Then take the text I'm about to paste and audit it hard — flag every banned pattern, every banned word, every tonal AI-ism. Rewrite each flagged section. Then audit the rewrite. Keep going until it passes.

props.title: It's not [X], it's [Y].
props.subline: one file · ban the pattern · sound human
